title: SoilGrids250m: Global gridded soil information based on machine learning
adjudication_reason: The paper reports "Fitted variable importance plots" (Fig 6) generated directly from ranger and xgboost's built-in importance (no mention anywhere in the text of "permutation," "SHAP," "Boruta," or "conditional" importance), i.e. the default impurity/gain-type measure for both packages. This ranking is used to state specific, non-hedged conclusions: "spatial pattern of soil pH is primarily influenced by precipitation and surface reflectance," "depth emerges as the most important covariate," and "DEM-parameters...emerge as second-most dominant covariates" for soil types/textures. Corroboration offered is only qualitative ("largely correspond with conventional soil survey knowledge"), a handful of raw bivariate scatter plots (Fig 7, not partial-dependence/ALE controlling for other covariates), and overall cross-validated R-square (which validates predictive accuracy of the full model, not the selected feature ranking) — none of which meets the bar of permutation/SHAP/PDP/conditional-importance/held-out validation of the ranking itself. The mismatch the authors themselves note (lithology visibly shapes predictions yet falls outside the top-15 importance list) is consistent with the classic impurity-importance artifact of under-weighting lower-cardinality categorical covariates relative to fine-grained continuous ones like precipitation/temperature/reflectance, so the bias could plausibly have altered this specific, stated ranking-based conclusion.

title: Soil nutrient maps of Sub-Saharan Africa: assessment of soil nutrient content at 250 m spatial resolution using machine learning
adjudication_reason: The paper fits ranger random-forest models over huge covariate stacks (hundreds of thousands of pixels, dozens of correlated remote-sensing/climate layers) and reports "Top ten most important covariates per nutrient, reported by the ranger package" (Table 2) and a "variable importance plot" (Fig. 11); nowhere does the text specify permutation, SHAP, conditional (party/cforest), or debiased importance, and the emphasis on computational feasibility ("MLA's can demand excessive computing time... limit prediction modeling to ranger and xgboost") is consistent with the cheaper default impurity/variance-reduction importance rather than the costlier permutation variant. The ranking is used to state central, assertive conclusions: "soil pH is clearly most important covariate for Na, K, Ca, Mg, Al," "Precipitation... distinctly comes as the most important covariate overall," and, for crop yield, "the most influential predictors... are: crop type, selection of nutrients and micro-nutrients (Mn, Zn, Al, B, Na)... This proves that producing maps of soil nutrients is indeed valuable for modeling agricultural productivity" -- the latter being effectively the paper's headline justification for its mapping effort. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the selected feature set is reported anywhere; the only quantitative checks given (OOB RMSE, cross-validated R-square) validate overall predictive accuracy, not the covariate ranking itself. The authors' own hedge -- that "because soil nutrients are heavily cross-correlated... relatively high importance of Mn and Zn could also indicate high importance of P, B, Fe and/or S" -- is an informal acknowledgment of exactly the correlation/interaction confound that drives impurity-importance bias, but it is a caveat, not a corroborating analysis, and it leaves the confident core claim (soil-pH/precipitation/nutrient dominance) intact. All three CONFIRMED criteria are therefore met.

title: African soil properties and nutrients mapped at 30 m spatial resolution using two-scale ensemble machine learning
adjudication_reason: The paper builds its headline claims directly on Random Forest (ranger) variable-importance plots ("The combined variable importance plots derived using Random Forest... reveal that... climatic images such as SM2RAIN... and CHELSA bioclimatic images... are the most important covariates" and "Sentinel-2 B11, B09 and B12, DTM vertical depth and Landsat SWIR1 are overall the most important"), with no mention anywhere in the text of permutation, SHAP, conditional, or PDP-based importance -- only ranger's default (impurity/variance-reduction) importance is described. This ranking is explicitly load-bearing for the abstract's conclusion, the stated 25% budget allocation to Sentinel-2 processing, and forward-looking recommendations (e.g., anticipating the LSTM mission based on MODIS LST importance, prioritizing peatland/nutrient sampling). The only corroborating evidence offered is (a) overall model accuracy/CCC/R2 improving when 30m covariates are added, which per the rubric does not validate a ranking, and (b) a preliminary univariate/few-band correlation-based R2 screening (Fig. 1) that only partially overlaps with the eventual RF-derived top bands (e.g., B04 tops the correlation analysis but not the final RF list; B09 tops RF importance but not the correlation screen) and does not cover the climatic-covariate ranking claim at all. No formal permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or held-out validation of the specific selected/ranked feature set is presented, so the flag survives: impurity importance is used, its magnitude/ranking is central to stated conclusions, and corroboration is inadequate.

title: Global mapping of potential natural vegetation: an assessment of machine learning algorithms for estimating land potential
adjudication_reason: The paper never names its importance method explicitly (no "permutation," "Gini," or "impurity" anywhere in the full text), but the workflow (ranger RF fitted via caret on datasets up to 1.5 million points and 180 covariates, with heavy emphasis on computational cost/time throughout) strongly implies the fast, built-in impurity importance rather than the far costlier permutation importance was used; permutation importance at this scale would have been computationally prohibitive and would have been worth mentioning given how much the paper dwells on compute limits. Ranking is clearly central to the paper's conclusions: the abstract and Conclusions section state verbatim which covariates are "the most important predictors" for each of the three case studies, and the Discussion builds a substantive ecological claim (that vegetation distribution is climate-dominated rather than lithology/landform-dominated) directly from lithology "not amongst the top 20 most important predictors," while also interpreting rank magnitude ("the decline in variable importance was, however, gradual"). Corroboration is limited strictly to predictive-accuracy metrics (TPR/AUC/RMSE/R2, spatial cross-validation) plus post-hoc ecological plausibility argument; there is no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the selected/ranked feature set anywhere in the paper. Since the ranking itself is never independently validated and it underwrites a real ecological conclusion, the flag holds.

title: A first-generation pediatric cancer dependency map
adjudication_reason: Methods describe a scikit-learn "Random forest regression" model with no mention anywhere in the text of permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance; the Ext. Data Fig. 9b/c legends explicitly state "the y-axis shows the feature importance as calculated by the predictive model," i.e. RandomForestRegressor's default impurity/MDI importance. This ranking underlies specific stated conclusions: "BCL2L1 expression was the most important feature in predicting MCL1 dependency," "the top predictive feature for MDM2 dependency was RNA expression of EDA2R... as a surrogate marker for functional p53," and "Predictive feature modeling for HDAC2 highlighted RNA expression of its known target FUCA1. This suggests that lower expression of FUCA1 indicates pediatric cell lines with high HDAC activity." The only corroboration offered is (a) bivariate scatter/linear-fit plots of the single top feature versus dependency score (Fig. 3c; Ext. Data Fig. 8b) and (b) overall model accuracy via 5-fold CV Pearson correlation between predicted and observed CERES scores. Neither validates that the reported feature actually outranks other correlated candidates in the multivariate importance computation: per the stated rubric, overall model accuracy does not validate the selection, and a bivariate plot of only the winning feature cannot demonstrate it beat competing features in a fair (bias-corrected) comparison. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional-importance analysis appears anywhere in the full text, methods, or supplementary table descriptions (Supplementary Table 6 just reports "feature importance" per top feature from the same RF models). The MCL1 functional follow-up (CRISPR knockout, S63845 inhibitor sensitivity) validates that the MCL1 dependency itself is real, not that BCL2L1 specifically (versus other correlated BCL-2-family transcripts or confounders) is the true top predictor. All three CONFIRM criteria are therefore met: genuine impurity importance use, centrality to stated conclusions, and lack of adequate corroboration.

title: RaFAH: Host prediction for viruses of Bacteria and Archaea based on protein content
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly states "variable importance was estimated using the impurity method" and Table S1's importance ranking is used to name "the most important predictor" (an Rz-like lysis protein) and identify the "50 most important proteins," which is then used to support the central claim that "RaFAH learned to predict virus-host associations based on proteins that are directly involved in virus-host molecular interactions." This ranking-based mechanistic claim is corroborated only by qualitative biological plausibility (these protein types are known to be involved in host range) and by held-out validation of overall model accuracy (F1/precision/recall on Test Set 2), not by permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out predictive validation restricted to the selected top-feature subset. Since impurity importance is used, its magnitude/ranking is central to a stated biological conclusion, and no adequate corroboration of the ranking itself exists, all three conditions for CONFIRMED are met.

title: Satellites can reveal global extent of forced labor in the world’s fishing fleet
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly computes feature importance as "the unbiased corrected Gini index, a measure of how well individual features do at reducing node impurity" — this is an impurity/Gini-type measure (a bias-corrected variant per Nembrini et al. 2018 that addresses split-count bias, not the superlinear/interaction/nonlinearity biases at issue here), not a permutation, SHAP, or conditional-importance measure. The resulting ranking (engine power, max distance from port, voyages/year, avg daily fishing hours) is used to support substantive causal-flavored claims in the Results text: "forced labor appears to cluster in similarly sized vessels," consistency with "expert opinion" and ILO indicators, and that vessels "may actively avoid scrutiny." No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation of this specific feature ranking is reported anywhere in the text; the paper's only "robustness checks" and 10-fold CV validate overall model classification performance (F1/recall/detection prevalence) and the number of vessels flagged, not the importance ranking itself. Because all three conditions (impurity-based importance used, ranking central to a stated conclusion, no adequate corroboration of the ranking) are satisfied, the flag is confirmed rather than refuted.

title: Increased ozone pollution alongside reduced nitrogen dioxide concentrations during Vienna’s first COVID-19 lockdown: Significance for air quality management
adjudication_reason: The paper trains its BAU/deweathering random forests entirely through the rmweather wrapper around ranger, and never mentions permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance anywhere in the text (verified by exhaustive keyword search of the fulltext); it reports only train/test correlation coefficients (r) as "predictive performance," which the rules explicitly say does not validate a feature ranking. The impurity-based ranking is then used to draw specific, stated conclusions: it is invoked as one of the paper's stated secondary contributions ("the importance of taking meteorology into account... is shown"), it is used to claim the RF models are demonstrably capturing real chemical/physical processes ("demonstrating the random forest models' interpretability nature"), and most concretely it supports a novel, counter-to-expectation scientific claim — that "RH is, on average, more important than Tair" for ozone, explicitly flagged as valuable for "future studies" on O3 sensitivity to meteorology — plus a discussion-section claim (Sec 3.5) that wind dominates NO2 while RH/Tair dominate O3/Ox, used to explain differing magnitudes of lockdown effects. None of these ranking claims are corroborated by permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE shape analysis, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the selected ranking itself, so all three conditions for CONFIRMED are met.

title: Mast cell‐based molecular subtypes and signature associated with clinical outcome in early‐stage lung adenocarcinoma
adjudication_reason: Section 2.8 only says a "randomforest" model was built (via the R randomForest package, Liaw and Wiener 2002) and Fig. 6's caption reports a single unlabeled "importance of ranking" with no mention of permutation, SHAP, conditional importance, or type=1 testing; the R randomForest package's node-purity (Gini/IncNodePurity) measure is computed by default even without the extra importance=TRUE permutation step, so the far more likely candidate is impurity importance, consistent with the first pass. This ranking is explicitly load bearing for the paper's central mechanistic claim ("TP53 and CSMD3 were the most important gene mutations," "TP53 was the most important mutation," and in the Discussion "we further identified TP53 as the most critical mutation," which is then wired into a TP53 to c MYC to mast cell narrative). The only independent check offered is a Mann Whitney U test (Fig. 5F) showing TP53 mutated patients have higher risk scores than wild type — this validates that TP53 alone associates with the score, but it says nothing about the relative ranking against CSMD3 or other mutated genes, does not use permutation/SHAP/PDP/conditional importance, and is not held out predictive validation of the selected feature set, so it does not meet the corroboration bar for the ranking itself. All three confirmation conditions are therefore satisfied.

title: Resting state connectivity best predicts alcohol use severity in moderate to heavy alcohol users
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly uses ranger's impurity importance ("Feature importance was evaluated using an impurity measure based on standardized response variance...scale.permutation.importance...set to FALSE"), and Table 2's ranked list of specific connectivity features (BG within-network, ASN-Sensorimotor, vDMN-vDMN, etc.) is used to support the central conclusion that specific SN/DMN/ECN/BG/sensory connections (not just the rs-fMRI modality generally) drive the 33% explained variance and corroborate the addiction-cycle neurocircuitry model. The only corroboration offered is (a) held-out R2/RMSE validation of the whole rs-fMRI model (aggregate predictive performance, not the individual feature ranking) and (b) a purely qualitative scatter plot (Fig. 3) of top features vs AUDIT in the validation sample with no reported statistics. Critically, the authors themselves concede this gap, writing "we do emphasize the need to focus on the results from the validation sample and interpret cautiously findings from the primary sample (i.e., the feature importance rankings), as the latter are not tested for generalizability." No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance was computed for the individual features. All three confirmation criteria are met, so the flag is CONFIRMED rather than refuted.

title: How Have Global River Widths Changed Over Time?
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly defines importance as impurity based: "Parameter importance is measured in terms of node impurity and calculated as the decrease in the residual sum of squares that results from splitting the response variable... A higher node impurity (or parameter importance) indicates that the parameter is more important." This ranking is central to the paper's headline conclusions, repeated in the abstract, key points, and discussion: "the most important factor driving temporal variations in river width is the climate for free-flowing rivers, and is soil condition for human-impacted rivers," and "Elevation is the most important parameter influencing river width variability." I searched the full text for permutation importance, SHAP (the one "shap" hit is actually "shape"), cross-validation, out-of-bag/R2 reporting, or held-out validation of the selected feature set, and found none. The only auxiliary tool is ALE plots, which the paper itself says are used only "to show how the river width variability and trend change with each of them" (visualizing functional shape/direction), not to independently validate the magnitude or rank order of importance. The paper even flags its own exploratory status ("further investigations are needed to explain why/how climate and soil erosion impact river width"), and its pre-selection step (choosing one "most important" parameter per correlated subcategory before the final RF) also relies on the same biased impurity importance, compounding rather than correcting the issue. All three CONFIRMED criteria are met: genuine impurity-importance use, centrality of the ranking to stated conclusions, and absence of adequate corroboration.

title: Soil carbon in the world’s tidal marshes
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly sets ranger's variable importance to "impurity" (Gini/variance-reduction) and reports it in Fig. 4, with no other importance method used anywhere in the text (grep confirms zero hits for permutation, PDP, or conditional importance; the only "shap" hits are substrings of "shaped"/"shapefile"). The ranking and magnitude are central to stated conclusions: "Soil depth was the most important driver of SOC density," elevation is discussed as "also an important variable" with a causal-sounding explanation, and critically the finding that "temperature... had similar relative variable importance as many other covariates" is used to argue against prior literature that temperature strongly drives SOC. The only "corroboration" offered is an overall R2 of 0.59 from spatial cross-validation, which validates predictive accuracy of the whole model, not the selected feature ranking, and the authors themselves flag the interaction confound without addressing it methodologically ("there is uncertainty in this assessment as underlying interactions between variables are not captured"). All three CONFIRMED criteria are met: impurity importance is genuinely used, its ranking/magnitude is central to a stated conclusion (depth as top driver; temperature as a weak/non-driver), and no permutation/SHAP/PDP/conditional-importance/held-out-ranking validation is provided.

title: Modelling the effects of environmental conditions on the acoustic occurrence and behaviour of Antarctic blue whales
adjudication_reason: The Methods section describes both permutation and impurity-based importance, but the actual reported metric in Figs 9 and 12 (the source of every "main/moderately/least important predictor" ranking) is explicitly captioned "Relative variable importance is measured by residual sum of squares and expressed relative to the maximum" — this is the mean-decrease-in-node-impurity (RSS) measure, i.e., classic MDI/impurity importance, not permutation importance. These rankings are central: the abstract itself states "SBACC, latitude and distance from the nearest Antarctic shores were the main geographic predictors" and "SSH, SST, and chlorophyll-a were the most important environmental predictors," and the Discussion builds biological interpretation directly on the "least important" status of month and day/night. No permutation importance values, SHAP, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the selected/ranked feature set is reported; the only validation metrics (AUC, RMSPE, Spearman rho, 5-fold CV/LGOCV) assess overall predictive accuracy of the fitted model, not the correctness of the impurity-based variable ranking, and the partial-dependence-style response curves (Figs 8, 10, 11) show relationship shape but do not corroborate importance magnitude or order. All three confirmation criteria are therefore met, so the flag cannot be refuted from the text.

title: Machine learning-assisted identification of bioindicators predicts medium-chain carboxylate production performance of an anaerobic mixed culture
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly and exclusively uses mean-decrease-in-Gini importance (the randomForest package default) to select the 15 top ASVs for HRT classification and, separately, the top-15 ASVs per process parameter (non-HRT bioindicators); no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance appears anywhere in the text. The headline conclusion, stated in both the abstract and results ("Four inferred bioindicators... suggest their relevance to the higher carboxylate productivity"; "we hypothesize that species represented by these four ASVs determined the increased C6/C8 productivities"), is derived purely from these four ASVs recurring across two separate Gini-based rankings (HRT classification and non-HRT productivity regression). Corroboration is inadequate under the stated bar: the metagenomic MAG analysis only shows the taxa genetically CAN perform relevant reactions (biological plausibility, not a statistical validation of the ranking), and the authors' own admission is telling ("the genetic potential alone does not guarantee that the respective metabolic process is performed"). Critically, the one held-out predictive test that specifically targets the disputed four-ASV selection found predictability "relatively poor" when using only these four bioindicators (Additional file 1: Fig. S14) — the opposite of corroboration — while the RRMSE~4.6% validation that does perform well applies to the broader 15-ASV HRT-bioindicator set, not to the narrower four-species claim. Hence all three CONFIRMED conditions are met.

title: Ensemble learning for detecting gene-gene interactions in colorectal cancer
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly uses Gini Index impurity importance for random forests ("the Gini Index importance of a feature was estimated based on the impurity of splitting testing samples on the tree node using the feature") and an analogous split-improvement-based (not permutation-based) importance for GBM. This ranking is squarely central: the 44-SNP candidate set, the headline claim that "SNP rs3760948 from gene ARRDC5 was ranked the highest by both random forests and GBM," and all downstream interaction/enrichment analyses derive directly from this impurity-based ranking cutoff. The only "validation" offered is (a) agreement between RF-Gini and GBM's own impurity/improvement-based score, two estimators that share the same class of impurity biases rather than providing independent corroboration; (b) univariate PLINK p-values, which several selected SNPs actually fail (e.g., rs11185516, p=0.669, rank 125,071/186,251), used by the authors to argue these are pure-interaction signals rather than as a check on ranking validity; (c) permutation testing of information gain, but only among the 44 already-selected SNPs to test specific pairwise/three-way interaction hypotheses, not permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the selection/ranking itself; and (d) functional enrichment/literature overlap, which is biological plausibility, not statistical corroboration of the ranking. Since the study specifically seeks interaction-driven, non-additive signal, it is exactly the regime where impurity importance's tendency to inflate variables involved in interactions could most plausibly have distorted which SNPs made the top-44 cut, and no method on the adequate-corroboration list was applied.

title: Machine learning-based chemical binding similarity using evolutionary relationships of target genes
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly computes ranger's random-forest variable importance defined as "a total decrease in node impurity" (classic Gini/MDI impurity importance), not permutation importance or SHAP. This ranking is used to draw a stated conclusion in the "Contribution of evolutionary information" section: Family, Target, and PFAM are ranked as high-importance sources while TS-SMART, TS-TIGR, TS-PRINT, and TS-Gene3D are ranked low, explicitly attributed to "low model performance...and target coverage" which is precisely the coverage-confounded scenario the bias flag describes, since PFAM/Family/Superfamily annotate over 74% of targets versus under 50% for the low-ranked sources. The only corroboration offered is a post-hoc, hedged ("probably", "we think", "might") correlation between each single feature's own held-out AUC (Table 1) and its Gini importance rank, which is exactly the kind of overall model-accuracy correlation the rubric says does NOT validate an importance ranking; there is no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or an ablation-style held-out test of the selected high-importance feature subset. Because the ranking supports a specific interpretive claim about which evolutionary databases matter and is not adequately corroborated by an independent importance method, the flag is confirmed.

title: Prediction of Physical Frailty in Orthogeriatric Patients Using Sensor Insole–Based Gait Analysis and Machine Learning Algorithms: Cross-sectional Study
adjudication_reason: The Methods explicitly state "Gini impurity was used to rank the variables in order of their importance" within the RF-based recursive feature elimination, and Figure 2 orders the 9 selected features "ranked by Gini-Impurity." The Discussion builds a substantive clinical claim directly on the magnitude/ranking of this impurity importance: it states the model "weighted stride length equally important as gait speed...in terms of their classification importance measured by the Gini impurity" and uses this specific ranking comparison to argue stride length is a more robust clinical parameter than the previously favored gait speed, concluding "stride length in addition to gait speed might be a valuable clinical parameter to identify patients with physical frailty." No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance is used anywhere in the paper. The only validation performed is overall AUROC/accuracy of the downstream KNN and RF classifiers trained on the whole 9-variable selected set (0.801/0.919 AUROC) — this validates that the feature set collectively predicts well, but per the stated rule this does not validate the internal ranking claim that stride length and gait speed are comparably important, which is exactly the analysis-specific bias impurity importance is prone to (correlated/continuous variables can be inflated relative to others). All three CONFIRMED criteria are met.

title: Role of intratumoral and peritumoral CT radiomics for the prediction of EGFR gene mutation in primary lung cancer
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly computes Random Forest feature importance via "the mean decrease in the Gini index" (Methods, ref. 29; Table 5 footnote "Measured using the mean decrease in the Gini index"), a textbook impurity/MDI measure, and ranks 32 mostly-continuous radiomic features plus 4 clinical features by it (Table 5, Supplementary Table 2). The Discussion then builds substantive biological interpretation directly on that ranking: it identifies LAHGLE as "the most important peritumoral feature" and the 90th percentile as "the most important" intratumoral radiomic feature, and constructs mechanistic explanations (vascular convergence, honeycombing/emphysema, ground-glass opacity) tied to their rank position relative to the other 30 features — i.e., the magnitude/ordering of impurity importance is load-bearing for a stated conclusion, not incidental. The only corroboration offered is a univariate Mann-Whitney/Fisher significance test showing these two specific features individually differ by EGFR status (p<0.001) — this validates directionality/non-nullity for two features, not the relative ranking or magnitude among the full feature set, and is not permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out predictive validation of the selected top-10 set. Since GLSZM/GLCM/first-order features are continuous, high-cardinality variables prone to impurity-importance inflation relative to categorical clinical covariates and to features involved in interactions, the specific "most important" designations used to anchor the biological narrative are not adequately corroborated, so all three conditions for CONFIRMED are met.

title: Classification of Molecular Subtypes of High-Grade Serous Ovarian Cancer by MALDI-Imaging
adjudication_reason: Methods 4.6 states explicitly: "feature selection was performed on the training set using Gini importance, the inherent ranking of features within the decision trees of a RF" with a "25% cut-off," and Results 2.5/2.6 report specific named proteins (H1.2, ACTA2, HSPA8, H2A, HSPD1) as "the highest relevance" or "one of the most predictive genes," with the Discussion building a biological narrative specifically around H1.2 being "highly discriminative." This is genuine impurity/Gini importance driving named biomarker claims, so ranking magnitude is central to a stated conclusion (identification of candidate protein markers), not just an incidental byproduct. The only validation offered is held-out mean AUC (0.983-0.999) of classifiers built on the whole 135-feature Gini-selected set, which validates that the selected SET predicts well but does not corroborate the internal ranking or the specific claim that ACTA2/HSPA8/H2A/HSPD1 or H1.2 individually rank above other features (Figure 3/5B spatial maps are illustrative, not a formal permutation/SHAP/PDP/conditional-importance analysis). No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance is used anywhere in the paper, matching the first-pass finding, so the ranking is not adequately corroborated.

title: Structural variants identified using non-Mendelian inheritance patterns advance the mechanistic understanding of autism spectrum disorder
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly uses Gini (impurity) importance from Ranger to select the top ten genes per cluster from an iRF classifier, then asserts specific genes literally "define" each subgroup (e.g., "an ASD-SV in the CTNNA2 gene defines cluster 1... whereas the CACNA2D1 gene... defines cluster 3"), and this is showcased in the abstract as a headline result ("use an explainable artificial intelligence approach to define subgroups demonstrating their use in the context of precision medicine"). The only support for this specific gene selection is a heatmap of the same frequency data the importance score was computed from, plus literature lookups tying the named top genes to unrelated phenotypes (startle response, cardiac arrhythmia); there is no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/conditional importance, or held out predictive validation of this particular top ten per cluster selection. The GO/eQTL/RNA seq validation cited by the first pass screen pertains to a separate, broader 1,116 gene core ASD SV set from an independent enrichment analysis, not to the Gini ranked cluster defining genes, so it does not corroborate this specific ranking. All three CONFIRMED criteria are met: genuine impurity importance use, centrality of the ranking to a stated headline conclusion, and lack of adequate corroboration of that ranking.

title: Multilevel predictors of climate change beliefs in Africa
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly states it computes "variable importance" using "the corrected Gini method" (citing Nembrini et al. 2018), which is a bias-adjusted variant of impurity/MDI importance that only fixes the cardinality/many-splits bias, not the super-linear magnification, nonlinearity-versus-skew confound, or interaction-attribution biases the flag targets, so it is still genuine impurity importance. Ranking/magnitude of this importance is central to the paper's headline claims, e.g. "worsening perceptions of agricultural conditions are the main predictor for climate change risk perception" and "Local changes in climate conditions are among the most important predictors across all models. Overall, they are more important than access to news, political ideology, or demographics," which drive the abstract's key results and Table 1's cross-model importance ordering. The only corroboration offered is partial dependence plots, which the authors themselves use solely "to illustrate the magnitude and direction of the direct effects of significant predictors" (i.e., shape/direction of individual effects), not to independently re-derive or validate the relative ranking across predictors; there is no permutation importance, SHAP, conditional importance, or held-out predictive validation of the selected/ranked feature set (the reported 63-80% "prediction accuracy" figures are whole-model accuracy, not validation of the ranking). All three criteria for CONFIRMED are met.

title: Fish grabbing: Weak governance and productive waters are targets for distant water fishing
adjudication_reason: The Methods section describes their "variable importance (VI) scores" as a "relative measure indicating how often a variable was used for a split in the final RF models" using the ranger package, with no mention of setting importance="permutation" — this is standard impurity-based (Gini/variance-reduction) importance, not permutation importance or SHAP. The ranking and magnitude of this VI measure is genuinely central to the paper's headline claims: "Governance effectiveness is the most important governance indicator in fixed gear, longliner and tuna purse seiner DWF, for the latter two it is the most informative across all variables" and "Tuna purse seiners are the only gear type in which governance indicators dominantly exceed biophysical variables in their Variable Importance scores," which directly underpin the Conclusion's paradigm-challenging claim that "governance performance indicators can be more important in explaining effort in some DWF gear or species specific fleets... than traditionally used ecological factors such as primary productivity." Corroboration is limited to a random-noise-variable threshold (which only screens informative vs. non-informative predictors, not correcting relative magnitude bias among real, differently-scaled predictors) and ALE plots (which show direction/shape of effects, not validity of the magnitude ranking itself). No permutation importance, SHAP, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the selected feature ranking is reported, so per the stated criteria the ranking is not adequately corroborated.

title: Predicting Clinical Remission of Chronic Urticaria Using Random Survival Forests: Machine Learning Applied to Real-World Data
adjudication_reason: The paper never names its importance metric explicitly (no mention of "Gini," "impurity," "permutation," "SHAP," or "VIMP" anywhere in the text), but the workflow (a ranger random survival forest, iterative reduction from 1,876 to 176 features, explicit note that "statistical significance testing imposes a substantial computation burden," and a bare "importance of predictors can be calculated on the basis of the model") is consistent with the fast, tree-split-based (impurity-type) importance rather than the far costlier repeated-permutation importance over ~1,900 candidate variables; no alternative rigorous importance method is ever cited. The magnitude/ranking of that importance is genuinely central to stated conclusions: variables are explicitly labeled "among the 20/50 most important predictors" and the Discussion and Conclusions build a pathogenesis narrative directly on this ranking ("The potential for the characteristics identified as important... to help predict time to clinical remission raise intriguing questions regarding the pathogenesis of CU," and Conclusion point 3: "provide insights into factors involved in the pathogenesis of the condition"). Corroboration is thin: only the single continuous variable age receives a partial dependence plot (Supplementary Fig. E1); the many binary/categorical top-ranked predictors (asthma, allergic rhinitis, chronic pulmonary disease, smoking status, LTRA/PPI/antihistamine use) receive no permutation importance, SHAP, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the selected feature set — the cohort-stratified summary tables merely describe how already-selected "important" features trend across outcome quartiles, which is circular and does not independently validate the ranking. Because impurity-type importance is plausibly used, its magnitude/ranking is load-bearing for a stated (if secondary) conclusion about disease pathogenesis, and corroboration is inadequate for all but one variable, the flag survives adversarial review.

title: Not just form, not just meaning: Words with consistent form-meaning mappings are learned earlier
adjudication_reason: The paper's own analysis code (stats/fsc_analyses_objAoA.R in the cited GitHub repo) calls ranger(...) with importance = "impurity" explicitly, confirming genuine Gini/variance impurity importance, not permutation importance. The Results section makes explicit magnitude/ranking claims from these impurity plots that function as stated findings supporting the robustness of the effect: "PSC ld, in particular, is slightly more important than concreteness and more important than most other predictors" and "PSC ld is more important than iconicity in predicting AoA and ranks just below concreteness." No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance is computed for the RF models; the corroboration actually present is linear-regression significance/direction (not magnitude), GAMs (non-linearity check), a random form-meaning-correspondence permutation test (a null-distribution check unrelated to RF variable importance), and ranger's OOB r.squared improvement from adding PSC alone (validates that PSC's marginal contribution is real, but not that its impurity-importance rank relative to concreteness/frequency/morphology/PND is trustworthy). Because the predictor set mixes continuous, binary, and skewed count variables known to bias impurity importance, and the specific comparative ranking claims are uncorroborated by any bias-robust method, all three CONFIRMED conditions are met.

title: What’s hot and what’s not: Making sense of biodiversity ‘hotspots’
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly states variable importance "was based on node impurity" (ranger package), with no permutation, SHAP, or conditional importance reported anywhere in the text. The impurity-based ranking is central to a headline conclusion: "Regional factors...were important predictors of diversity...This suggests that regional- to ecosystem-scale processes chiefly determined assemblage structure...with small-scale habitat characteristics, such as substrate playing an important but subordinate role," and "Chlorophyll, phosphate and differences in dissolved oxygen concentrations ranked the most important gradients associated with anthropogenic pressure" — directly feeding the paper's conservation-scale argument. The only corroboration offered is partial dependence plots restricted to the top impurity-ranked variables (circular, since PDP selection depends on the same ranking), and the authors themselves caution these "may not be realistic and lead to misleading results in the case of strongly correlated variables" — exactly the scenario present here (chlorophyll/phosphate/DO form a correlated eutrophication gradient, and continuous high-cardinality covariates like depth/shear stress compete against categorical substrate variables). A qualitative citation to Somerfield et al. (2009) using an unrelated method (species relatedness) offers weak, non-quantitative support but does not validate the specific importance ranking or magnitudes. No permutation importance, held-out validation of the selected feature set, or conditional importance is reported, so the ranking is not adequately corroborated.

title: Environmental predictors impact microbial-based postmortem interval (PMI) estimation models within human decomposition soils
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly states "Importance reports the decrease in mean square error (MSE) for each feature" and Fig 5 caption says "Top 25 model features determined by variance of responses in Ranger" — this is impurity/variance (MDI-type) importance from the Ranger RF package, not permutation importance. The ranking is central to the Discussion's biological narrative: "the most important model feature...was the phylum Firmicutes," "EC appeared to be most influential," and these rankings are used to build causal/successional claims (the "Clostridium Effect," anaerobic vs. aerobic taxa dynamics, EC as a proxy for ammonium). The feature set spans wildly heterogeneous predictors (35-5195+ taxa of skewed compositional relative-abundance data mixed with continuous environmental measurements), exactly the setting where impurity importance is biased toward high-cardinality/skewed features. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance is reported anywhere in the text (searches for "permutation," "impurity," "SHAP," "conditional," "cforest" all return zero hits besides the MSE-decrease description). The only "validation" present (S3 Table, train vs. test R²) assesses overall predictive accuracy, not the specific selected/ranked feature set, so it does not meet the adequate-corroboration bar. All three confirmation criteria are met.

title: Modeling Physiological Predictors of Running Velocity for Endurance Athletes
adjudication_reason: The paper's sole variable-selection/ranking mechanism is XGBoost's built-in feature-importance heat map (Figure 3), a gain/split-based (impurity-analog) measure, not permutation importance or SHAP. The ranking is explicitly central: the Conclusions list "we found the strongest predictors of running velocity" as a headline finding, and Section 4.1 builds an extended physiological narrative directly on the ranking magnitudes ("VO2 at RCP and maximal VO2 were the most important parameters...a lower impact of VO2 at AT was noted"; "[La-] ... more impact from sub-maximal levels ... than maximal values"; "HR was one of the variables with the lowest impact"). The only validation offered is 10-fold cross-validation, Bland-Altman analysis, and RMSE/MAE of the final regression equations' overall predictive accuracy; this validates the fitted model's fit, not the magnitude or ordering of the XGBoost importance scores themselves, and overall test-set accuracy does not validate a ranking. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance is reported anywhere in the text, so all three confirmation conditions are met.

title: The current status and shortcomings of stereotactic radiosurgery
adjudication_reason: The paper fits an R `ranger` random forest and reports "the variables of importance" in "arbitrary units (AU)" (Figure 4) with no mention of permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance anywhere in the text (verified by full-text search); combined with the authors' unfamiliarity with importance-bias literature (they cite only Breiman/Ho/Dietterich, not Strobl et al.), this is consistent with the common default of impurity (Gini/variance) importance rather than a bias-corrected measure. The ranking is unambiguously central to the paper's conclusion: "field size was the most important variable while collimator was the least important variable... field size is the driving force behind failures, and cone collimators are simply correlated with field size," which directly drives the clinical recommendation to pay "particular attention" to "smaller field sizes." Critically, this causal-attribution claim is corroborated only by narrative reasoning and univariate chi-squared tests (a different, confound-blind method not on the approved corroboration list) - there is no stratified/conditional analysis, no permutation importance, no PDP, and no held-out validation of the feature ranking itself to disentangle the acknowledged field-size/collimator correlation. Since field size is the only continuous predictor competing against several categorical predictors (collimator, algorithm, machine, energy, year), this is exactly the scenario where impurity importance mechanically inflates a continuous variable's rank over a correlated categorical one, so the flag stands.

title: Toxicity Assessment of Mixed Exposure of Nine Perfluoroalkyl Substances at Concentrations Relevant to Daily Intake
adjudication_reason: The paper uses a scikit-learn RandomForestClassifier (n_estimators=1000, max_depth=3) and reports its default "feature importance" (i.e., impurity/Gini/MDI) to rank 189 metabolites, stating "Of the 189 compounds, 114 had a feature importance of zero... The results annotated 5-aminovaleric acid as the most important compound" and "5-(Carbamoylamino)pentanoic acid, which had the second highest contribution." This exact ranking is then used to build a specific mechanistic narrative in the Discussion, tying the top feature to a GABA-receptor/Chrna4 hypothesis ("may be associated with increased gene expression of Chrna4") — a nontrivial, ranking-dependent interpretive conclusion, so the ranking is central to at least this part of the paper's story. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the specific top-feature selection was performed; the only quantitative validation offered (90% test-set classification accuracy) validates overall model accuracy, not the identity/order of the selected features, which per the stated criteria does not count as adequate corroboration. All three conditions for CONFIRMED are met.

title: Predicting Time to Death After Withdrawal of Life-Sustaining Measures Using Vital Sign Variability: Derivation and Validation
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly ranks RSF features "based on their importance values," citing Ishwaran's high-dimensional RSF variable-selection paper and Nembrini et al.'s "The revival of the Gini importance?" as its methodological basis (the latter specifically concerns Gini/impurity importance and its biases), indicating an impurity-type (MDI) measure drove the feature-removal step. This ranking is central to a stated conclusion: "all clinical features were removed from the combined model" during optimization, and the Discussion states "clinical features used in this work added no additional benefit," a claim used to justify design choices for future models. The only corroboration offered is that the final combined (post-removal) model achieves AUC 0.78-0.80 on the held-out validation cohort; per the task's own criterion this does not validate the ranking/removal decision itself, since no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or explicit ablation re-adding clinical features was reported. With 71 highly correlated continuous variability metrics competing against six clinically mixed-type (including binary) features, impurity importance's known scale/cardinality and correlation-driven inflation could plausibly have overstated variability importance and understated clinical feature importance, so the flag survives adversarial review.

title: Killer whale acoustic patterns respond to prey abundance and environmental variability around the Prince Edward Islands, Southern Ocean
adjudication_reason: The paper fits RF classification models and reports a single scaled 'feature importance value' per predictor that is used to rank predictors ("Wind speed was the most important predictor... while sea surface height, chlorophyll-a and sea surface temperature were moderately important"), and this ranking is central to the ecological interpretation (wind noise masking communication space, seasonal coincidence with low wind speed). The only statistical treatment applied is Altmann et al.'s permutation-based p-value test, which yields a significance verdict (asterisks p<0.05 vs NS) but by construction does not re-derive or correct the magnitude/ordering of the underlying importance scores, matching exactly the split the audit describes: significance is robust, ranking/magnitude is not. The paper never explicitly names the base measure as "Gini" or "impurity," but the workflow (default randomForest/ranger importance output, a GVIF collinearity check run specifically "to produce accurate indices of variable importance," and citation of Altmann's classic Gini-importance-bias-correction paper) is most consistent with a conventional impurity-type importance being the reported metric. The only corroboration offered is (i) partial-effect plots (figure 6 a-h) showing each predictor's marginal shape, which validates direction/shape but not relative magnitude or rank across predictors, and (ii) overall model AUC/calibration on a held-out test set, which the rubric explicitly excludes as validation of a selected ranking. No permutation importance, SHAP, or conditional importance was computed as an independent check on the ranking, so the magnitude/ranking claims driving the paper's ecological conclusions are not adequately corroborated.

title: Rich cities, poor countryside? Social structure of the poor and poverty risks in urban and rural places in an affluent country
adjudication_reason: The paper fits ranger random-forest models and reports a single, unqualified "variable importance" measure with no mention of permutation anywhere in the text; the only methodological caveat cited is Strobl and Zeileis (2008) on the known bias of impurity-type importance toward variables with more categories/continuous scale, which the authors address only by recoding continuous variables to categorical (a partial workaround, not a switch to permutation/conditional importance). The text also explicitly narrates the classic MDI interaction-credit artifact: "sector of occupation... is less important in the analysis covering only characteristics of the social situation, [but] it gains importance in the full model because of its interaction with the variables measuring the opportunity structure." The magnitude/ranking of this importance is the direct basis for the paper's central claims ("the social situation variables outperform... social groups," "opportunity structure... have lower predictive power") and the resulting urban/rural policy recommendations. The only validation offered is overall classification-error confusion matrices (Table 2), which check aggregate model accuracy, not the correctness of the specific feature ranking or selection; no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the ranked feature set is reported anywhere in the manuscript.

title: Prediction of Suicide-Related Events by Analyzing Electronic Medical Records from PTSD Patients with Bipolar Disorder
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly computes importance as "the decrease in node impurity weighted by the probability of reaching that node" (classic MDI/Gini-style importance), ranks all 92 features by this magnitude in Table 3, and uses that ranking both to select the "top 30" features for the final model and to frame the headline clinical claims (e.g., Age at rank 1 with 0.141, Trazodone rank 5, disease categories 5/3/1/11 as the emphasized comorbidities). The abstract's specific medication claims ("strong indicators for no SREs" / "predicted the onset of SREs") are downstream of this impurity-based selection step, and the paper's own limitations section concedes impurity importance "can be misleading for high cardinality features and continuous variables (age and ED visits)" — precisely the two features ranked highest — yet the only mitigation offered is a distribution check for those two continuous variables (Figure 4) plus a chi-square/proportion test (Table 5) computed on outcome groups that are themselves defined by TreeInterpreter contributions from the same impurity-built trees, which is circular rather than independent. The paper explicitly performs held-out accuracy validation of the selected 30-feature subset (Table 4), which per the rubric does not validate the ranking itself, and no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or genuine conditional-importance analysis is reported anywhere in the text. All three CONFIRMED conditions are therefore met: real impurity importance is used, its ranking/selection is central to the stated clinical conclusions and treatment-substitution recommendations, and no adequate corroboration of that ranking is provided.

title: DNA Methylation in INA, NHLH2, and THBS4 Is Associated with Metastatic Disease in Renal Cell Carcinoma
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly computes impurity based importance (mean decrease in Gini and times_a_root via randomForestExplainer, with ranger splitrule = gini) and uses it to declare which specific CpG loci are the "most important predictors" ("6 of the 7 (86%) INA, 2 of the 4 (50%) NHLH2, and 2 of the 7 (29%) THBS4"). This ranking directly underwrites a stated translational conclusion in the Discussion: that "a subset of 10 candidate loci from the total of 18 ... already permits the identification of tissue samples with a metastatic disease state ... with good accuracy," framing a minimal biomarker panel. The only validation offered is the 0.73 test-set accuracy of the full 18-locus random forest model; no separate model was fit or tested on just the 10 "important" loci, and no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance was used to corroborate which loci matter or their rank order. Per the rubric, overall model accuracy on the full feature set does not validate a ranking-based feature subset, so the ranking underlying the 10-of-18 loci claim is uncorroborated and the flag is confirmed.

title: Kinome inhibition states and multiomics data enable prediction of cell viability in diverse cancer types
adjudication_reason: The paper's own code (github.com/gomezlab/kinotype_viability, src/build_ML_models_expression_regression/make_model_pred.Rmd, lines 73 and 102) sets `set_engine("ranger", ..., importance = "impurity")`, confirming the random forest uses impurity/variance-reduction importance exclusively; the paper text never mentions permutation importance, SHAP, or Gini explicitly but the code settles it. This ranking is central to a headline conclusion: the abstract states they "identified a set of kinases, several of which are understudied, that are strongly influential," and the Results section names CSNK2A2, PIP4K2C, CAMKK2 and DYRK1B as "influential" purely on the basis of their position in the top-25 impurity importance plot (Fig 3C) — these four names appear exactly once in the paper with no follow-up. No corroboration method is used: Fig 6's experimental validation tests only overall dose-response prediction accuracy on held-out cell lines/compounds, not the identity or ranking of important features, and there is no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or feature-ablation/knockdown test of the named kinases anywhere in the text or code (grep for "permutation"/"SHAP" in the repo returns no relevant hits). All three CONFIRMED criteria are therefore met.

title: Biological knowledge-slanted random forest approach for the classification of calcified aortic valve stenosis
adjudication_reason: The paper ranks genes purely from the RF-construction process itself ("we calculated the number of times each gene was selected in the 500 trees" / "Frequency of the 20 top genes selected in 500 trees"), i.e. a tree-internal, impurity-driven selection-frequency measure rather than permutation importance or SHAP; at each node the winning split among mtry candidates is the one with the largest impurity decrease, so this count inherits the same super-linear/interaction-charging distortions as classic MDI. This ranking is squarely central to the paper's substantive claims: ATP6V0D2 is declared "the main gene used to classify the cases," a top-20 list is presented as the disease-relevant gene set, and the paper explicitly concludes "knowledge-slanted RF could be more easily interpreted by users because the prediction can be attributed mainly to associated genes." The only checks offered are (a) post-hoc univariate ANOVA of expression differences on the already-selected top genes (conditional on selection, not an independent ranking validation) and (b) comparing median RWR-network rank of the two methods' top-20 lists (357.5 vs 564.5), neither of which is permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out predictive validation of the selected feature subset; overall model accuracy/AUC (59.3-92.6%) is reported but that validates classification performance, not the gene ranking. Hence all three CONFIRMED criteria are met.

title: Using Machine Learning to Evaluate the Value of Genetic Liabilities in the Classification of Hypertension within the UK Biobank
adjudication_reason: The paper builds RF models with the "ranger" package and derives importance via caret's "vip" function without ever mentioning permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or any bias correction (no hits for "permutation," "impurity_corrected," "Strobl," "cforest," etc. in the full text) — the standard, unqualified use of ranger+caret varImp defaults to impurity/Gini-based importance, and nothing in the text suggests otherwise. The ranking is unambiguously central to the paper's headline conclusions: the specific claim that "genetic liability for two lipid traits (TC and LDL) was found to improve the classification of hypertension" rests entirely on TC/LDL genetic liability landing in the top-10 importance ranking, and the Discussion draws a substantive biological inference from diabetes NOT appearing in that ranking ("did not prioritize diabetes as an important feature... may align with existing inquiries in the literature"). The only corroboration offered is that a model built from the RF-selected top-10 features achieves AUC 0.71 on the held-out test set — but per the stated rubric this is overall model accuracy/AUC on a test set, which explicitly does NOT count as validation of the ranking itself (it doesn't test rank order, feature-level robustness, or whether swapping in excluded features like diabetes would change conclusions). No permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional-importance corroboration exists anywhere in the text, so the flag survives adversarial review.

title: Predicting the presence of depressive symptoms in the HIV-HCV co-infected population in Canada using supervised machine learning
adjudication_reason: I read the full text plus the supplementary appendix (Additional file 1, a docx I fetched and parsed). Neither the main text nor Appendix B/C ever mentions "permutation," "SHAP," "conditional importance," or any bias-corrected importance method — they only say "the RF importance metrics were generated" via ranger/caret and describe importance generically ("The algorithm also provides importance metrics to provide an idea which variables were the most influential"). Given the complete absence of any permutation/SHAP language (which authors invariably flag explicitly when used, since it costs extra compute and is a deliberate methodological choice), the unqualified "importance" here is best read as the default impurity (Gini/variance) importance from ranger — satisfying criterion (1). Criterion (2) is unambiguous: the Discussion and Conclusions treat the top-5 ranked predictors as substantive findings ("The five most important predictors are related to two main themes—i. SES ... and ii. overall health status"), and the Conclusion restates this ranking as a key result. Criterion (3): the only evaluation reported is overall model discrimination (AUC/sensitivity/specificity/calibration) on the held-out test set — which the audit rules explicitly say does NOT validate the ranking itself — plus a regression sub-analysis (R²=0.5) that also does not touch the importance ranking. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional-importance corroboration of the specific top-5 selection appears anywhere, including the appendix tuning tables (Table S2), which list only B/mtry/D/S hyperparameters with no importance-mode disclosure or validation step. I could not find grounds to refute the flag; all three conditions for CONFIRMED are met.

title: Machine learning model of the catalytic efficiency and substrate specificity of acyl-ACP thioesterase variants generated from natural and in vitro directed evolution
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly computes "Feature importance scores for each residue position... based on the total decrease in node gini-impurity averaged over the 500 trees" (ranger package), ranks residues by these scores, and uses that ranking (via incremental feature selection) to select the "top 22 residue positions." The central biological conclusion, "the majority of highest scoring residues (17 out of 22 residues) reside within the N-terminal hot-dog structure," and the claim that 5-16 new candidate residues (e.g., I52, L59, A63, L64, V67) govern substrate specificity, depend directly on which specific residues the gini-importance ranking placed at the top. The only statistical treatment applied is the ranger "importance_pvalues" (Altmann) permutation-of-response test with FDR correction, which addresses whether an importance score is significantly non-zero, not whether the relative magnitude/ranking among correlated or interacting residue positions is unbiased. The subsequent 10-fold cross-validation only reports overall recall/specificity/MCC of nested cumulative-feature models to find a performance plateau, which is exactly the kind of "overall model accuracy on a held-out set" that the rubric says does not validate a ranking (no random-subset baseline or alternative importance/conditional-importance method was used to check whether a different set of 22 residues would perform as well). Only 6 of the 22 selected residues have independent prior experimental (site-directed mutagenesis) confirmation; the other 16, including all novel claims, rest solely on the uncorroborated gini-importance ranking, and the discussion's appeal to structural plausibility (proximity to the hot-dog domain/active site) is a post-hoc narrative fit rather than a formal corroboration method (permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance).

title: Monthly and Diel Acoustic Occurrence of Four Baleen Whale Species in South African Waters
adjudication_reason: The abstract's headline conclusion rests directly on RF importance magnitude/ranking ("SSH and SST...were the most important predictors"), and Section 3.5/4.4 build a full most/moderately/least-important tier list for each species purely from the scaled importance percentages in Figure 6b. The Methods (2.6) never specify importance.mode; they cite the classic 'randomForest' package (whose default importance() is Gini/MeanDecreaseGini) fit "through ranger for speed," and pair it only with Altmann et al.'s permutation-of-response significance test — which is the standard corrective device applied to impurity/Gini importance in the ecology literature and, per the task's own framing, only certifies importance != 0, not rank reliability. Figure 6a's "influence" curves are PDP-like shape plots shown alongside importance, but the text never uses their amplitude to cross-validate the Gini-based ranking, no permutation (Breiman-type) importance, SHAP, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the selected feature subset is reported, and known bias drivers (chlorophyll-a's skew, hour's 24 categorical levels, evidenced by the GVIF/aGSIF table) are present. So the ranking is central and uncorroborated, and impurity-style importance is the best-supported reading of what was actually computed, though the paper's silence on importance.mode leaves some residual uncertainty on that specific point.

title: Whole blood RNA sequencing identifies transcriptional differences between primary sclerosing cholangitis and ulcerative colitis
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly trains random forests with "average Gini impurity increase" importance (ranger), then uses "average Gini impurity decrease" to weight genes for GSEA ("we used the top 50% cumulated importance genes as input and weighted those by feature importance") and to name the "top 10 most important genes" (Table S19). This importance-weighted GSEA produces a specific headline biological claim not recoverable from the independent DESeq2 differential-expression analysis: for UC vs. CON and UC vs. PSC, "cytoplasmic translation" is the only significant enriched term, and ribosomal/mitochondrial genes (COX6B1, MT-RNR2, RPS9) are elevated to top-importance status even though they do not appear among the 74 DEGs found by the Wald test for UC vs. CON (whose top hits are S100A12/S100A9/C19orf59). This ribosomal/translation/mitochondrial signature becomes a substantive Discussion conclusion ("cellular biosynthesis and metabolism are generally upregulated in UC ... some have a high importance in the random forest classification model. This was not observed in PSC"), i.e., a conclusion whose content is driven by the magnitude/ranking of Gini importance rather than by the orthogonal DE test. The only validation offered anywhere in the paper is held-out AUROC of the full classifier (and cross-cohort AUROC replication for the UC-vs-CON model), which validates overall predictive accuracy, not the specific importance ranking or the top-50%-importance gene subset used for the pathway/gene-naming claims; there is no permutation importance, SHAP, conditional importance, or held-out validation of that selected feature set. Hence all three confirmation criteria are met.

title: Final weight prediction from body measurements in Kıvırcık lambs using data mining algorithms
adjudication_reason: Table 6 reports "Inc. node" (IncNodePurity) from R's randomForest package, the classic RSS-decrease impurity importance for regression, an MDI analog known to be biased toward wide-range/high-cardinality and correlated predictors (HW itself spans 6.5 to 68, an outlier range among the measurements, and predictors are highly intercorrelated, e.g., BH-CH r=0.862). The paper states this ranking as a headline conclusion ("Using the RF algorithm, the variables affecting body weight the most were CC, CH, and CD") and ties it to a practical recommendation for producers selecting predictors/genotypes. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation of this specific selection is performed; the only cross-checks are Pearson correlation and PCA, which are not on the accepted corroboration list and in fact conflict with the fine-grained RF order (HW ranks 4th in RF importance despite having the lowest correlation with SW, r=0.187, contradicting the impurity-based ranking). Since the ranking is genuinely impurity-derived, is central to a stated conclusion, and lacks the required type of corroboration, the flag holds.

title: Combining Environmental Variables and Machine Learning Methods to Determine the Most Significant Factors Influencing Honey Production
adjudication_reason: The paper computes 'feature importance' for the best RF model 'using the Ranger package' (Sect. 2.2) and plots it in Fig. 2 ('Feature importance based on Random Forest'), never specifying permutation mode, which in tidymodels/ranger workflows without other qualification is standardly the impurity (Gini/variance) mode. The specific top-3 ranking (T2M_2, PS_2, WS2M_1) is not a throwaway detail: Sect. 3.4 singles them out as 'the most significant predictors,' and the Discussion builds a mechanistic story directly from that order ('elevated temperatures and pressure levels in February may signal the onset of favorable growing conditions... wind patterns in January could impact foraging behavior'), so the ranking is load-bearing for a stated interpretive conclusion, not just the generic set of 18 Boruta-retained features. No adequate corroboration of this ranking exists: Boruta only performs zero/non-zero relevance detection against shadow features (it does not validate magnitude/order), and the only other check (Fig. S1, AUC vs. number of features across full/Boruta/top-6 models) is an overall-accuracy comparison across feature subsets, which the rubric explicitly excludes as ranking validation. A single garbled phrase ('...six most influential variables based on Shafigurepley importance scores (see Figure 2)') appears to be a corrupted reference to 'Shapley importance,' verified present verbatim in the official PMC/MDPI HTML as well, but it is never elaborated in Methods, no SHAP values/table/plot are shown anywhere else, and it is used to cite the same Figure 2 that is explicitly captioned 'based on Random Forest' -- so this reads as a leftover drafting artifact rather than a genuine, described SHAP corroboration. Given the ranking is explicitly invoked in a scientific interpretation and no real corroboration method is reported, the flag holds, with the caveat that the paper's silence on impurity-vs-permutation for the ranger call is inferred from common practice rather than stated outright.

title: Spatiotemporal patterns and drivers of soil organic carbon in black soil landscapes of Northeast China
adjudication_reason: The paper uses the Liaw-Wiener randomForest R package and reports a single "importance ranking...presented as percentages" (Fig 8) with no mention anywhere in the text of "permutation," "%IncMSE," "mean decrease accuracy," SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance — the complete absence of permutation-specific language, combined with the default behavior of randomForest() (IncNodePurity/impurity importance is computed automatically; %IncMSE requires explicitly setting importance=TRUE and is never referenced), makes it most likely this is impurity-based (IncNodePurity, i.e., Gini/MDI-analog) importance. This ranking is unambiguously central: the paper's headline finding "Precipitation emerged as the most influential variable affecting SOC" (based on 39.32% vs 17.83%/16.88%/13.03%) is repeated in Results, Discussion 4.1, and directly underwrites the policy recommendations in section 4.4 ("governments should carefully consider...support sustainable land management"). The only corroboration offered is bivariate Spearman correlation (which happens to reproduce the same rank order but is not on the accepted list of adequate methods — it doesn't test the RF's non-linear/interaction-based ranking or feature-selection validity) and overall test-set R²=0.78 (model accuracy, not ranking validation). No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance analysis of the RF's own feature ranking is reported anywhere in the text, so the ranking is not adequately corroborated per the stated bar, and the flag survives adversarial review.

title: Variations in Cycling Distances by Trip Purpose and Socio-Demographic Attributes: Implications for Spatial Scales to Assess Environmental Correlates of Cycling
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly states it used "an actual impurity reduction importance measure" from a ranger Random Forest, citing Nembrini et al. 2018 (a paper on bias corrected Gini/impurity importance) - this is genuinely an impurity family measure, not permutation importance or SHAP. The resulting ranking (trip purpose=100%, gender=21%, region=10%, state/employment/age=5% each) is the direct empirical basis for the paper's headline policy conclusion that "purpose specific buffer sizes need to be employed," and for the secondary claim that gender is the "second most important determinant," so magnitude/ranking is unambiguously central, not incidental. The only other statistical analysis present is univariate Wilcoxon/Kruskal Wallis tests (Table 3), but these show ALL six explanatory variables are statistically significant (p less than 0.001 or p=0.007) - they establish that each variable's marginal association is nonzero, not the relative magnitude/ranking among variables, so they cannot corroborate why trip purpose ranks about 5x above gender or why gender ranks about 2x above region. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held out validation of the selected feature ranking appears anywhere in the text. One mitigating nuance worth flagging: citing Nembrini et al. 2018 suggests they may have used ranger's bias corrected "impurity_corrected" variant rather than naive Gini/MDI, which would reduce (though not eliminate) cardinality driven inflation of the 3 to 4 level trip purpose variable relative to the binary predictors - but this detail is not confirmed in the visible text (only in an unavailable supplement), and even a corrected impurity measure is not among the adequate corroboration methods specified, nor does it address interaction charging or nonlinear effect conflation. On the visible evidence, none of the required corroboration types are present, so the flag stands.

title: Characterizing Families of Spectral Similarity Scores and Their Use Cases for Gas Chromatography–Mass Spectrometry Small Molecule Identification
adjudication_reason: Section 2.4 explicitly states relative variable importance in the random forest was determined using the Gini Index, and no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the selected feature ranking is reported anywhere in the paper (the only performance metric given is 100% overall test accuracy, which validates the classifier, not the importance ranking). The ranking is not incidental: the paper explicitly interprets the magnitude ordering ("the computed t-statistics of metrics is definitively the most important... the second-most important factor... is the overlap score") to conclude that "the four empirical cluster groups are largely determined by the discriminatory ability of each metric," and this ranking is then used to select t-statistic and overlap score as the "top three most important predictive factors" characterizing clusters in Table 1, which underpins the paper's core recommendation of which metric clusters/scores to use. Because the predictor set mixes continuous distributional statistics (t-statistic, overlap score, min/median/max) with categorical variables (family, type, bound direction), this is precisely the setting where impurity/Gini importance is known to be biased toward high-cardinality continuous predictors, so the ranking claim is not robust to that known artifact and is not corroborated by any alternative method.

title: Modelling microclimatic variability in Andean forests of northern Patagonia
adjudication_reason: The paper fits ranger() random forests and reports "Relative importance (%)" per predictor (Fig. 2, Table 2) without ever naming the importance type (impurity/Gini vs. permutation) or citing any bias-correction; feature selection itself was done by recursive elimination on CV R²/RMSE, not by an independent check of the importance ranking, so that RFE step validates which variables enter the model but not the reported magnitudes/order (e.g., TMIN-station "68.3%" ranked #1, macroclimate "ranging from 50 to 80%"). These exact magnitude/ranking numbers are then used directly in the Discussion to declare macroclimate the dominant driver and solar radiation/FAPAR/NDMI as "key predictors" — a central substantive conclusion of the paper. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the selected ranking is reported anywhere in the text, so the ranking is not adequately corroborated. Given the silence on importance type plus heavy reliance on ranger's default workflow across ~9,600 model fits (where impurity importance is the cheap, common default), the more likely reading is that unadjusted impurity/Gini-type importance drives these ranked percentage claims, satisfying all three CONFIRMED criteria.

title: Kinome state is predictive of cell viability in pancreatic cancer tumor and cancer-associated fibroblast cell lines
adjudication_reason: The paper's text only says variable importance was "gathered" (citing the vip package, Greenwell & Boehmke 2020) without naming the importance type, but the publicly linked analysis code (build_klaeger_screen_binary_predictions.Rmd in the paper's own GitHub repo, cited in the manuscript) shows the ranger engine is fit with set_engine("ranger", importance = "impurity") before calling vip() — this is classic Gini/MDI impurity importance, not permutation or SHAP. That ranking is central to a headline conclusion: the abstract itself states the models "identify a set of kinases whose behavior in response to perturbation drive the majority of viability responses... including the understudied kinases CSNK2A1/3, CAMKK2, and PIP4K2C," and a full Results subsection ("Novel, understudied targets of PDAC are identified") builds biological narrative around each kinase's rank position (e.g., "CSNK2A1/3 was identified as highly ranking," "CAMKK2...is a top feature found only in the P0119-T1 CAF"). No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance is used to check ranking stability; the only corroboration offered is (a) qualitative face-validity from recovering well-known targets (MAPK, AURK, EGFR) in the same top-30 lists, and (b) the Fig. 5 validation screen, which uses the full-feature model to predict binary viability on held-out compounds and reports overall ROC/PRC accuracy — it never restricts to the top-ranked feature subset or otherwise tests whether the specific rank/identity of CSNK2A1/3, CAMKK2, or PIP4K2C is robust, so per the stated criteria it does not count as validation of the selected feature set. All three CONFIRMED conditions are therefore met.

title: Impact of morphological traits and irrigation levels on fresh herbage yield of sorghum x sudangrass hybrid: Modelling data mining techniques
adjudication_reason: Table 6 explicitly reports RF importance as "Node Purity" (impurity/variance-reduction, i.e., MDI), and the Conclusion lists as a key finding an explicit ranking: "NDF, PH, and RFV for the RF algorithm," with an overall combined claim that "the most impactful factors are PH, RFV, and NDF" — so the ranking is central to stated conclusions.
