The only corroboration offered is comparing this ranking to importance measures from ALM (standardized regression coefficients), ANN (connection-weight sensitivity), and MARS (basis-function contribution) — none of which is permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out predictive validation of the selected feature subset. No such approved corroboration method is used anywhere in the paper. Given documented severe multicollinearity among the very top-ranked variables (NDF VIF=195.95, RFV VIF=286.4, PH VIF=44.0), the impurity-importance ranking is exactly the kind of result the known bias could distort, and it is not adequately corroborated per the stated criteria, so all three conditions for CONFIRMED are met.

title: IgG Antibody Responses to Epstein-Barr Virus in Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome: Their Effective Potential for Disease Diagnosis and Pathological Antigenic Mimicry
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly computes "mean decrease in the Gini index" averaged over 2500 random-forest runs to rank all 3054 antibodies, then uses both the magnitude ("overall average antibody importance was around 0.018" vs. top value 0.36) and rank position (e.g., EBNA1_430 "ranked in the 1866th place," EBNA6_0070 "not among the top most important antibodies") to conclude, in the paper's own Conclusions section, that "the role of EBV on eventual ME/CFS-related autoimmunity should be reconsidered under the lens of danger theory" — i.e., ranking/magnitude directly drives the central molecular-mimicry conclusion. The only corroboration offered is (a) a Spearman correlation between the same Gini importance values and sequence-homology scores (which reuses the potentially biased magnitudes as its input rather than independently validating them) and (b) held-out test accuracy of the final 26-antibody classifier (90%), which per the stated rubric validates that the selected set predicts disease status but does not validate the internal ranking/magnitude comparisons used to argue individual candidate antibodies were unimportant. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance was used, so the ranking claim central to the mimicry conclusion is not adequately corroborated.

title: Investigation of the Relationships between Coat Colour, Sex, and Morphological Characteristics in Donkeys Using Data Mining Algorithms
adjudication_reason: The RF model reports "Inc Node Purity" values (Table 3) — classic impurity/Gini-type importance from the ranger implementation — with no permutation importance computed despite ranger trivially supporting it. This ranking is stated as a headline finding and repeated verbatim in the paper's conclusion: "In order of importance, the variables affecting the body length in donkeys were Province, WH, and HR for the RF Algorithm," so the ranking is central to a stated conclusion. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance was applied to the RF model to validate this ranking. The only other evidence is comparison to different algorithms' own (also uncorroborated) importance metrics, and these actually disagree in a way that is consistent with the classic impurity-importance cardinality bias: CHAID, which uses adjusted-p-value significance tests for splits, never selects Province (a 13-level categorical) as a splitting variable at all, whereas the RF impurity metric ranks the same high-cardinality Province variable as the single most important predictor — exactly the pattern the impurity bias predicts (inflated importance for high-cardinality categoricals and highly correlated continuous traits like WH/HR, r=0.951). This disagreement across models is evidence of instability rather than corroboration, and none of the paper's cross-algorithm comparisons meet the required corroboration bar (permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the selected feature set).

title: Nowcasting tourist nights spent using innovative human mobility data
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly defines its "variable importance" as impurity-type MDI: "the reduction in sum of squared errors whenever a variable is chosen to split a node across every tree of the forest" (ranger's impurity importance), with no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance computed anywhere. This ranking is central: the Results section header "The predictive power of human mobility" leads with "It is clear that for 2020 and 2021 G has a greater importance compared to the other features," the Discussion attributes Luxembourg's fit to G being "the only important predictor," and the Abstract/Conclusion headline claim ("this novel data source is able to predict tourism demand with high accuracy," "We demonstrate that human mobility data from Google ... can be used to nowcast tourism demand") rests on attributing the model's success specifically to G rather than to the static tourism/population covariates. The only validation offered (Err_abs, Err_rel, Mod_Err, RMSE against Eurostat totals) is overall model accuracy of the full model with all features included, which per the audit rules explicitly does NOT validate the ranking/attribution to G specifically, since no ablation (model without G) or permutation-based check was run. The setup is a textbook bias scenario: G is a continuous, lockdown-skewed predictor being compared against categorical/static predictors (5-level vulnerability class, tourism typology, and a within-country-constant population), which impurity/variance-reduction importance is known to systematically inflate relative to lower-cardinality or static features, so the reported dominance of G could be an artifact of the metric rather than a genuinely larger causal/predictive contribution.

title: Haemosporidian Infection Risk Variation Across an Urban Gradient in a Songbird
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly runs a ranger random forest and selects "significant" predictors "based on corrected Gini impurity values," using a magnitude threshold (the absolute value of the lowest-importance variable) and reports the ranked importances (wing chord 0.494 > trash 0.38 > tables 0.375) in Figure 5. This ranking is directly load-bearing for a central conclusion: "wing chord length and human activity levels...were the most significant variables for predicting infection risk," generalized in the Discussion/Conclusion to "biotic factors—rather than abiotic environmental conditions—may play a more significant role in increasing infection risk." No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance appears anywhere in the text (only false-positive substring matches for "ale" and unrelated uses of "Shap-ing"); the only follow-up is an rpart classification tree built on the same full (non-held-out) data, which is itself another impurity-based method rather than an independent corroboration, and the reported 84.7%/86% "accuracy" is overall model accuracy, not validation of the selected feature ranking. All three confirmation criteria are met.

title: Groundwater and Remotely Sensed Phenology Reveal Vulnerability of Riparian Trees to Drought
adjudication_reason: The paper fits ranger random forests (500 trees, tuned mtry/min.node.size) and derives "Vimp" via the vivid package (Inglis et al. 2023), which for tree-ensemble models like ranger extracts the model's embedded (impurity-type) importance rather than an independent, bias-corrected metric; the text never mentions permutation importance, SHAP, or any bias-corrected alternative. This ranking is squarely central to the paper's headline conclusion, stated in the abstract, Section 3.3, and Discussion: "photoperiod dominated at the perennial site, while DTW was the controlling factor at intermittent sites," visualized in Figure 5 by node size (Vimp). The only checks offered are (1) a "sensitivity analysis" showing the top-three predictor identities (DTW, photoperiod, PDSI) are stable across alternative precipitation-lag specifications, which reuses the same importance metric rather than an independent method, and (2) overall variance explained (>90%), which the audit criteria explicitly exclude as validation of a ranking. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the selected feature ranking is reported anywhere in the main text or supplement, so the magnitude/ordering of photoperiod vs. DTW importance across site types is not adequately corroborated against the impurity-importance biases (non-linear/skew inflation, interaction misattribution) that could plausibly reshuffle which variable "dominates" at each site type.

title: Predictors of adjustment to life after service among Canadian military veterans
adjudication_reason: The paper explicitly computes RF variable importance via ranger's Gini/impurity method ("Node impurity was measured by the Gini index...to determine variable importance"), not permutation importance. The magnitude/ranking is central to the paper's core conclusions: it reports "the top ten most important predictors...in order of importance, were life satisfaction, mastery, mental health, age, BMI, social support..." and the discussion/clinical implications are built around this specific order (e.g., psychological variables ranking above demographic/military/health-behavior variables). The only follow-up analysis (multinomial logistic regression) is fit on the same non-held-out data using only the pre-selected top-5/top-10 variables already chosen by the impurity ranking; it never re-examines the excluded variables from the full 36-variable set, and is not permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance as required for adequate corroboration. This is exactly the scenario the bias flag targets, since impurity importance is known to be inflated for continuous/high-split-count variables (life satisfaction, age, BMI, income) relative to binary/categorical ones (deployment, sex, service branch), which the paper explicitly notes "did not emerge as important predictors."
